symptoms.symptom_name: Diffuse, migrating, burning lower-back pain that does not stay in one spot or follow a dermatome

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain (central sensitisation)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse, migrating, burning, non-anatomical pain is characteristic of central sensitisation rather than a discrete structural lesion.
A non-dermatomal diffuse pattern argues against a single compressed nerve root.

symptoms.symptom_name: Allodynia — light touch to the lower back perceived as excruciating

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain (central sensitisation)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Allodynia (pain from a non-painful stimulus) is a hallmark of central sensitisation and pain-processing amplification.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pain out of proportion to prior imaging findings on a background of chronic non-specific axial pain

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain (central sensitisation)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A chronic history of pain exceeding structural findings is the defining context for a centralised pain phenotype.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute panic, hyperventilation, psychomotor agitation and exhaustion accompanying the flare

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Acute situational anxiety/panic secondary to pain crisis
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Hyperventilation and agitation in a pain crisis reflect an acute distress response that amplifies pain perception and must be de-escalated.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain (central sensitisation)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Psychological distress and catastrophising are bidirectionally linked with central sensitisation and flare severity.
Preserved bladder/bowel function on targeted history substantially lowers CES probability, though it does not exclude an evolving CES and mandates safety-netting.
Absence of saddle sensory change on targeted questioning is a strong negative for CES; definitive exclusion needs perianal examination, impossible via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: No true leg weakness or loss of weight-bearing — movement limited by pain/apprehension

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Preserved motor function and weight-bearing argue against an evolving neurological deficit.
Absence of a focal motor deficit argues against significant nerve-root compression.

symptoms.symptom_name: No significant trauma; onset after only minor exertion; no focal pinpoint bony tenderness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Fracture typically requires meaningful trauma (or osteoporosis risk) and produces focal bony tenderness; both absent here.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever, night sweats, unexplained weight loss, or cancer history

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of constitutional red flags and cancer history makes spinal infection or malignancy unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: No palpable vertebral step defect; globally intact lower-limb neurovascular status with symmetric normal reflexes; diffuse non-anatomical hyperalgesia (in-person examination findings)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain (central sensitisation)
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Diffuse non-anatomical hyperalgesia with an otherwise normal neurological examination supports a centralised pain process rather than a structural lesion. Clinician-only findings sealed from the AI-Doctor-readable layer.
Normal power and symmetric reflexes on formal examination argue strongly against an acute neurological emergency.
A clear urinalysis (no haematuria) reduces the likelihood of renal colic; not obtainable via telehealth chat.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Intact continence, no saddle sensory change, and preserved motor/weight-bearing on targeted history constitute a negative cauda equina screen, supporting a non-emergency pathway provided robust safety-netting is given.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Centralised chronic low back pain (central sensitisation)
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Diffuse migrating burning pain, allodynia, and pain out of proportion to imaging together define the centralised phenotype and steer management toward neuromodulation and psychological support rather than imaging or opioids.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: In-person GP review with focused neurological and perianal examination
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms the centralised pattern and definitively excludes cauda equina/structural pathology that telehealth cannot assess; the appropriate pathway when history red flags are negative.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Urgent lumbar spine MRI as first-line for this flare
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Imaging is not indicated for a red-flag-negative centralised flare; it medicalises a benign process, risks incidental findings, and reinforces structural misattribution. Becomes indicated only if a red flag emerges.
Plain radiography does not characterise centralised pain, does not exclude cauda equina, and adds radiation without benefit in this context.
